summary: Our farm is located about fifteen miles north of Miami, and we've been living in Florida for over twenty years. Miami is the nearest big city to us, and although we live in a rural area, Miami represents the urban life that contrasts with our farm. We moved here after the 1926 Miami hurricane, which destroyed many buildings, including our previous home. Miami is part of the larger area we call home, but we prefer the quiet life on the farm.

Our farm is about six miles west of Hollywood, Florida, and our children attend the Catholic school there. Hollywood is the closest town where we send the kids to school, and it’s part of our daily life. The community there is important to us, especially for church and education. We’ve been living near Hollywood for about eight years now since we settled on this farm.
cues: children attend Catholic school in Hollywood

summary: The 1926 Miami hurricane was a turning point for us because our current house was built after that storm. The hurricane caused so much destruction that it made us rebuild with stronger materials. Our house is very solid, with all connections bolted and embedded in cement to withstand such storms. Living in Florida, we learned the importance of building to endure hurricanes like the one in 1926.

The Everglades are just nearby, and my son Richard used to go out there in an old Model T Ford roadster to catch wild animals. He studied taxidermy as a hobby and mounted all sorts of creatures from the Everglades, like alligators, snakes, and birds, which decorate our living room. The Everglades have been a source of fascination and adventure for our family, especially for Richard’s taxidermy work.

Richard used to go out in an old Model T Ford roadster when he went hunting in the Everglades. He would come back with the rumble seat full of live alligators and other animals he caught. That old Model T was essential for his adventures and collecting specimens for his taxidermy hobby. It was quite a sight to see him return with the car loaded like that.

Our big police dog Fritz is a great-grandson of Bin Tin Tin, the famous movie dog. We paid $200 for Fritz when he was a puppy, and now at seven years old, he’s a strong and protective dog, weighing about 150 pounds. Fritz is a valuable watch dog for our farm, even though he once chased and killed all our calves. His lineage from Bin Tin Tin makes him quite special in our eyes.

We own about 50 head of fine registered cattle, including some Jersey cattle. These cows are part of our dairy business, which we’ve been running for about ten years. The Jerseys, along with Guernseys, provide the milk we bottle and sell. Raising these cattle is a big part of our farm life and livelihood here in Florida.

Along with Jersey cattle, we also have Guernsey cattle on our farm. Both breeds are registered and contribute to our dairy production. We’ve had some tough times with calves, especially last year when our dog Fritz killed them all. Still, the Guernseys are an important part of our farm’s success and milk supply.

Two of my daughters live in New York City; one is married with children, and the other, Margaret, is a trained nurse working in a big hospital there. They visit us whenever they can, but I rarely get the chance to visit them. New York City represents a different life from our farm here in Florida, but we’re proud of how well they’re doing.

Frances, my oldest daughter at home, is engaged to an engineer who lives in Fort Lauderdale. It’s close enough that we know the area well, but Frances plans to stay here on the farm for now to help me. Fort Lauderdale is part of our family’s connections in Florida beyond the farm.

Three of my oldest boys work together in a large airplane factory on Long Island, while my oldest son James is an aviator on the west coast. Long Island represents the industrial and aviation opportunities that have drawn my sons away from the farm. They’re all interested in aviation, and we’re proud of their careers there.
cues: three boys working in airplane factory

summary: Although Chevrolet wasn’t directly mentioned in our daily life, we do have a milk truck that my husband John drives on his route during the winter when the boys are in school. The truck is essential for selling honey and milk from our farm. Chevrolet trucks are common in this area, and I imagine ours is a Chevrolet, helping us manage the dairy business efficiently.
